title: Chapter 13: Extraction
content: Hong answered on the third ring.
"You need to leave. Now." Ren kept his voice low, pacing the length of his room. Dawn was still an hour away. "The entity traced you through the tea house. You have less than forty-eight hours."
A pause. Then Hong's voice—calmer than it had any right to be: "I know."
"You *know*?"

"I felt it. Last night. Something brushed against my awareness—not the full possession like before, but a... tasting. Like a tongue testing the edge of a wound." Her breath was steady but shallow. "I've been expecting it since the tea house. You don't walk away from something like that without consequences."
"Where are you?"

"Still in the city. I moved after our meeting—different hotel every night, cash only, no phone GPS. But it doesn't matter, does it? If the entity can find me through dimensional fracture, no amount of physical hiding will work."

She was right. The Abyss didn't track by signal—it tracked by resonance. Anyone who'd been touched by the entity's consciousness carried a faint dimensional signature, like a radioactive tracer. Physical distance was irrelevant.
"Then we need to change the signature."
"How?"
Ren ran the calculations. The Salvation System was already filtering options:

`COUNTERMEASURE: Dimensional signature masking`
`METHOD: Array interference — a resonance frequency that disrupts the tracking signature`
`REQUIREMENT: A live array source close enough to the target to create interference`
`AVAILABLE OPTIONS: (1) Jade pendant (Xue's), (2) Jade token (Qian keepsake), (3) Estate's ambient array field`
`PROBLEM: Options 1 and 3 are inside the Liang estate. Option 2 is with you but has limited range (5 meters).`
"I have an artifact that can mask your signature. But I need to get it to you, and I need to stay close enough for it to work."
"Five meters close?"
"Five meters close."

Silence. Then Hong laughed—a dry, humorless sound. "So your rescue plan is to stand next to me in a city full of Abyss-hunting spore carriers with an artifact the size of a coin and hope for the best?"
"My rescue plan is to get you inside the Liang estate."
Dead silence.
"You're insane."

"The estate's array field extends across the entire property. It's the strongest dimensional interference zone in Crystal City. Inside the estate boundary, the entity's tracking can't find individual resonance signatures—it's like trying to hear a whisper in a hurricane."
"And the Liang family's security? The guards? Your wife?"
"I'll handle that."
Another pause. Longer this time. Ren could hear her breathing—the measured rhythm of someone making a decision that would change everything.

"There's something else," she said finally. "The reason they're coming for me isn't just the tea house. It's what I found. Qian Delun—he didn't just detect the heartbeat. He triangulated it. He mapped the entire array network beneath the Liang estate before they took him. And he sent the map to me."
Ren's heart stopped.
"You have a map of the portal array?"

"I've had it for two years. Encrypted, on a drive I've kept in a safe deposit box. I couldn't read it—I'm not an array practitioner. But Qian was. If he mapped it before they took him, that map shows every node, every connection, every vulnerability in the Patriarch's life's work."
The Salvation System:

`🟢 CRITICAL INTELLIGENCE ASSET`
`A complete map of the dimensional portal array would reveal:`
`1. All 7 node locations (including the 3 currently unknown)`
`2. The integration sequence and dependencies between nodes`
`3. Vulnerabilities—points where the array can be disrupted or corrupted`
`4. The activation conditions (blood sacrifice requirements, timing windows)`
`PRIORITY: Acquire this map. It changes everything.`
"Get to the northern gate of the estate. 6 AM. I'll have you brought in as a household services consultant—legitimate cover, security-cleared. Can you access the safe deposit box before then?"

"Already have the drive on me. I've been carrying it for six months, waiting for the right moment to give it to someone who could use it." A breath. "Zhou—if you're who I think you are, based on what that thing called you in the tea house... then you're the first person in two years who might actually be able to read this map."
"6 AM. Northern gate. Don't be late."
Getting Hong into the estate required every scrap of political capital Ren had accumulated.
He went to Xue first. Not with the full truth—she wasn't ready for that—but with enough.

"I have a contact in the city who's been tracking dimensional anomalies. She's being hunted by the same force that's infected Feng. If we don't bring her in, she'll be taken within forty-eight hours—and with her, intelligence that could help us understand what your father is building."

Xue was in her study, the morning light making her look younger and more tired than her corporate armor allowed. She'd been up all night too—Ren could see the shadows under her eyes, the slight tremor in her hand when she reached for her tea.
"Another stray," she said. "First my brother, now your contact. You're collecting people who see what I refused to look at."
"Would you prefer I left them to the Abyss?"
The word—*Abyss*—hung between them. Since the garden conversation, they'd both been using it. Naming the enemy made it real. And real was terrifying, but real could be fought.

"Bring her in," Xue said. "Household consultant, security clearance, renewable weekly. But Ren—she's *your* asset. When my father asks—and he will ask—I'm telling him she's part of your intelligence initiative. That means any problems she causes are your problems."
"Understood."

Hong arrived at the northern gate in a taxi, carrying a single suitcase and a look of grim determination. The household security gave her a thorough check—physical search, identity verification, the works. The drive was in a hidden pocket sewn into her jacket lining, and the guards missed it. Either they weren't looking for data drives, or Hong had disguised it beautifully.
She was escorted to a small guest room in the east wing—two doors down from Ren's. Close enough for the jade token's five-meter masking radius.
He visited her as soon as the guards left.
"The drive." He held out his hand.

Hong pulled it from her jacket—a slim black rectangle, unmarked, the kind of generic storage device you could buy at any electronics store. But when the Salvation System interfaced with it, the data structure bloomed like a flower made of light:

`DATA DETECTED: Array cartography — Liang Estate dimensional network`
`COMPLEXITY: Extreme (pre-dynastic notation, requires specialist interpretation)`
`COMPLETENESS: 94% — all major nodes and connections mapped`
`ANALYSIS TIME: 48-72 hours for full interpretation`

Ninety-four percent complete. Qian Delun had mapped almost the entire array before they took him. The missing 6% was probably the inner sanctum—the most heavily shielded area, where the primary anchor and the portal mechanism itself resided.

But 94% was enough. It was more than enough. With this map, Ren could identify every node's location, every connection between them, and—most critically—the specific vulnerabilities that would let him sabotage the integration process without triggering alarms.
*The Bronze Bell's calibration can be corrupted. I just needed to know HOW—and this map will tell me.*
"Qian Delun was a genius," Ren said softly.
"He was a kind man who made tea float and treated old women's arthritis with warmth from his hands." Hong's voice was rough. "And they took him for it."
"I'm going to find out what happened to him. I promise."
"You keep making promises, Zhou. One day you're going to have to keep them."
He tucked the drive into his pocket alongside the jade token—two artifacts, one ancient and one modern, both carrying secrets that could change the trajectory of the war.
"I intend to."
One thousand eighty-one days.
